name: Cadborosaurus
description: Cadborosaurus, often nicknamed "Caddy," is a sea serpent said to inhabit the Pacific Coast of North America, particularly the waters off British Columbia. The creature is described as having a long neck, a horse-like head, a serpentine body, and flippers. Proponents believe Cadborosaurus is a surviving type of prehistoric marine reptile, possibly related to plesiosaurs or other ancient sea creatures. Sightings and anecdotal evidence form the basis of the belief, with some blurry photographs and alleged eyewitness accounts being presented as proof. The name 'Cadborosaurus willsi' was proposed by Francis Kermode, director of the British Columbia Provincial Museum, after Cadboro Bay in Greater Victoria, though it was never formally recognized scientifically. Mainstream science remains highly skeptical, attributing sightings to misidentification of known marine animals such as oarfish, basking sharks, whales, or seals swimming in a line. Skeptics also point to the lack of credible photographic or video evidence and the absence of any physical remains or DNA evidence to support the existence of the creature. The few purported specimens have been debunked as known animal remains. Despite the lack of scientific validation, Cadborosaurus remains a popular figure in cryptozoology and continues to capture the imagination of those interested in unexplained phenomena and undiscovered species. The enduring appeal of Cadborosaurus lies in the hope of discovering a large, unknown animal in a relatively well-explored region.
subtitle: The elusive sea serpent of British Columbia

claims.claim: Cadborosaurus is a surviving species of prehistoric marine reptile.
claims.evidence: Similarities in description to plesiosaurs and other long-necked marine reptiles.
claims.criticism: Lack of fossil evidence in the region indicating plesiosaur survival. Observed characteristics can be attributed to known marine animals.

claims.claim: Eyewitness accounts consistently describe a creature matching the Cadborosaurus description.
claims.evidence: Numerous reported sightings over decades along the Pacific coast.
claims.criticism: Eyewitness testimony is unreliable and prone to misinterpretation. Descriptions are often vague and inconsistent.

claims.claim: Some photographs and videos depict a creature that cannot be easily identified as a known animal.
claims.evidence: Several blurry photographs and videos exist, claimed to show a long-necked creature in the water.
claims.criticism: The quality of these images is poor, and they are easily explained as misidentified known animals or fabricated hoaxes.

keyFigures.name: Edward Bousfield
keyFigures.role: Canadian marine biologist who investigated and proposed a classification for Cadborosaurus.

keyFigures.name: Paul LeBlond
keyFigures.role: Oceanographer who co-authored a book on Cadborosaurus.

keyFigures.name: Benjamin Radford
keyFigures.role: Science writer and skeptic who has critically examined Cadborosaurus evidence.

mythologyConnections: Indigenous legends of sea monsters along the Pacific Northwest coast
Similarities to traditional serpentine deities and creatures

timeline.event: Increased sightings reported, particularly in the Cadboro Bay area.
The name 'Cadborosaurus willsi' is proposed by Francis Kermode.
Alleged capture of a 'baby Cadborosaurus' inside a whale, later dismissed as a fetal baleen whale.
Continued sightings and occasional media attention, but no scientific validation.

sources.author: Radford, Benjamin

sources.title: Radford, Benjamin
